coredata.dc:title: State of charge estimation for Lithium-Ion battery cell considering the influence of aging parameters and operating temperature
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.dc:creator: Nguyen Van, Chi
Quang, Duy Ta
coredata.dc:description: This article presents a state of charge (SoC) estimation for Lithium-Ion batteries (LiB) based on first-order equivalent circuit model (ECM) that takes into account the aging parameters and operating temperature of the cell. First, the first-order ECM of the cell is proposed considering parameters of the aging process including state of health (SoH) solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer resistance, and charge transfer resistance. The parameters of this equivalent circuit model are established for a new cell at different operating temperatures. The model's physical parameters are identified based on experimental data from specific discharge/charge scenarios. Based on the ECM proposed, the SoC estimation algorithm for battery cells using the principle of Sigma Point Kalman Filter (SPKF) filter is applied. The use of the SPKF filter allows for consideration of the non-linearity of the relationship between open circuit voltage and SoC in the model. The parameters describing the cell aging process used in ECM permit representation more accurate of the dynamics of the cell change during operation. The estimated SoC for aged cells corresponding to different SoH values shows that the amount of SoC increase during constant-current charging, full-charging time, and average SoC increase rate during charging depend strongly the aging of the cell. SoC estimation results considering SoH will achieve higher accuracy, especially for cells used in practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: State of Charge
Lithium-Ion Battery
State of Health
Internal resistances
Sigma Point Kalman filter

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: First-order ecm of Lib cell considering the influence of aging parameters and operating temperature
The aging parameters of LiB cell
The proposed first-order ECM for LiB cell
Determining the relationship between OCV and SoC with respect to temperature
Determine the dynamic parameters of the model according to temperature
SoC estimation of cell using SPKF based on ECM of lib cell considering the influence of temperature and aging parameters
SoC estimation for LiB cell using SPKF based on ECM of LiB cell considering the operating temperature and aging parameters
Results of SoC estimation considering the influence of the aging

Results of SoC estimation of cell for charge cycle 1 corresponds to SoH = 87.04 %: a) Changes in voltage of cell, b) Changes in charge current, c) Changes in temperature, d) SoH of charge cycle 1, e) Resistances of charge cycle 1, d) Estimated SoCof cell.

Results of SoC estimation of cell for charge cycle 50 corresponds to SoH= 85.41 %: a) Changes in voltage of cell, b) Changes in charge current, c) Changes in temperature, d) SoH of charge cycle 50, e) Resistances of charge cycle 50, d) Estimated SoCof cell.

Results of SoC estimation of cell for charge cycle 100 corresponds to SoH= 70.25 %: a) Changes in voltage of cell, b) Changes in charge current, c) Changes in temperature, d) SoH of charge cycle 100, e) Resistances of charge cycle 100, d) Estimated SoC of cell.

Results of SoC estimation of cell for charge cycle 150 corresponds to SoH = 62.77 %: a) Changes in voltage of cell, b) Changes in charge current, c) Changes in temperature, d) SoH of charge cycle 150, e) Resistances of charge cycle 100, d) Estimated SoC of cell.
Comparing estimated SoC at charge cycles 1, 50, 100 150 correspond respectively to SoH = 87.04 %, 85.41 %, 70.25 %, and 62.77 %.
Comparing temperature at charge cycles 1, 50, 100 150 correspond respectively to SoH = 87.04 %, 85.41 %, 70.25 %, and 62.77 %.

row.entry: The amount of increase in SoC on CC mode

head.ce:title: State of charge estimation for Lithium-Ion battery cell considering the influence of aging parameters and operating temperature

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Thai Nguyen University of Technology, Viet Nam

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Vinh Phuc College of Economics and Technology, Viet Nam

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Vinh Phuc College of Economics and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Vinh Phuc College of Economics and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This article presents a state of charge (SoC) estimation for Lithium-Ion batteries (LiB) based on first-order equivalent circuit model (ECM) that takes into account the aging parameters and operating temperature of the cell. First, the first-order ECM of the cell is proposed considering parameters of the aging process including state of health (SoH) solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer resistance, and charge transfer resistance. The parameters of this equivalent circuit model are established for a new cell at different operating temperatures. The model's physical parameters are identified based on experimental data from specific discharge/charge scenarios. Based on the ECM proposed, the SoC estimation algorithm for battery cells using the principle of Sigma Point Kalman Filter (SPKF) filter is applied. The use of the SPKF filter allows for consideration of the non-linearity of the relationship between open circuit voltage and SoC in the model. The parameters describing the cell aging process used in ECM permit representation more accurate of the dynamics of the cell change during operation. The estimated SoC for aged cells corresponding to different SoH values shows that the amount of SoC increase during constant-current charging, full-charging time, and average SoC increase rate during charging depend strongly the aging of the cell. SoC estimation results considering SoH will achieve higher accuracy, especially for cells used in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:para:
LiB is known today as an essential storage for electricity in mobile electronic devices, electric vehicles, and distributed power systems. Due to their advantages over other types of batteries such as high-energy density, high charge efficiency, reduced self-discharge, high cell voltage, no memory effect, etc. LiB cells now are widely used in the field of energy storage [1–3]. With the carbon reduction strategy, amount of electric and hybrid electric vehicles in the world will explode in this decade. The global demand for LiB is expected to soar over the next decade with the required increasing from about 700GWh in 2022 to around 4.7 TWh by 2030. When using electric vehicles, the most important thing for customers is to know the amount of energy available in a battery at a specific point in time expressed as a percentage, or how far they can travel reliably and accurately at the present time. To monitor and control the performance and safety of the battery system, the battery management system (BMS) is installed and used to measure the physical states of cells, then calculate and inform users of the accurate SoC value of the LiB pack. SoC is an important parameter, that acts like a fuel gauge in a car representing the amount of remaining electric charge in the battery cell. SoC depends nonlinearly on many parameters such as current, voltage, operating temperature, and especially the degree of cell aging or the number of used charge/discharge cycles [4–6]. With the same SoC in percentage, the electric vehicle with the new LiB will be allowed to travel further than the one with the LiB older. For an older cell, the time to fully charge will also be shorter than that for a new cell. State of Health (SoH) defined by the formula (1) is used in the literature and also practice applications to represent the degree of aging of the cell [7–10].
(1)
SoH
k
=
Q
max
t
Q
rate
100 %

In formula (1),
Q
max
t
is the maximum capacity of cell that can be used at time k ,
Q
rate
is the nominal capacity of a new cell provided by the manufactory. When the LiB cell has been used for a certain period, the actual capacity
Q
max
t
will decrease in comparing with
Q
rate
, resulting in a decrease in SoH. The phenomenon of aging occurs naturally due to the electrochemical reactions inside LiB [13–15]. Fig. 1 shows how the SoH degradation of the cell is after 600 charge/discharge cycles. In the document [11,12] the authors introduce methods for accurately estimating the remaining capacity
Q
rate
of the aged cell based on anti-noise adaptive long short-term memory (ANA-LSTM) model and Gaussian process regression long short-term memory (GPR-LSTM) model.

SoC is an inside physical state parameter that cannot be directly measured in commercial LiB applications, it is only determined by calculation using the other measured parameters. Currently, there are two methods for estimating SoC: the first is methods that use Kalman filter, Extended Kalman filter, SPKF filters based on ECM or electrochemical model [16–18]; the second is data-based methods using machine learning based on neural networks, RNNs, FNNs, etc [19–21]. The advantage of using data is that training the network requires a lot of data, but the estimated SoC takes into account the aging characteristics in the data. The method of estimating SoC based on electrochemical models using filters allows for SoC estimation with consideration of the effects of aging occurring inside the cell, but the computational burden is very high due to its relation to many partial differential equations describing the electrochemical behavior inside the cell. The method of estimating SoC based on ECMs is currently widely used in commercial BMSs, because it has the advantage of less computational complexity, however, the model parameters for estimating SoC are almost fixed and mainly consider the temperature effect [22,23]. When LiB cells become aged, the model parameters will no longer be accurate, so SoC estimation will become less accurate and will not reflect the dependence of SoC on aging. This is the motivation for this research. This paper deals with a method for estimating SoC based on first-order ECM proposed that considers parameters reflecting the aging process of the cell including SoH, SEI layer resistance, and charge transfer resistance. The estimated SoC based on the SPKF filter for the corresponding aging cell for different SoH values show that SoC estimation considering SoH will achieve higher accuracy, the amount of SoC increase during charging and the average SoC increase rate during charging will be different corresponding to the different aging levels of the cell.

This paper is organized as follows: the introduction is written in Section 1, Section 2 describes the first-order ECM of LiB cells considering the influence of aging parameters and operating temperature. We present the estimation of SoC using SPKF in Section 3, some conclusions are shown in Section 4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: First-order ecm of Lib cell considering the influence of aging parameters and operating temperature
The aging parameters of LiB cell
ce:section.ce:para:
The aging phenomenon of cells in the operation process can be caused by many reasons including the thickening of the SEI layer, the corrosion of lithium, the loss of interaction during use, and the increasing of temperature of LiB during operation caused by the loss of free ions increases the internal resistance. Fig. 2 shows the electrochemical impedance model of the cell, where L represents the impedance at high frequency, W is the characteristic element of diffusion,
R
e
//
C
e
is the impedance at the medium frequency range with
R
e
being the SEI resistance, and
R
ct
//
C
ct
is the impedance at the low frequency range with
R
ct
being the charge transfer resistance [24–27].

Recent studies have shown that the two resistances
R
e
and
R
ct
are affected strongly by the aging process [25–27]. Fig. 3 is the plot of the changes in
R
e
and
R
ct
resistances according to the capacity degradation
Q
rate
of the SAMSUNG 16850 25R cell after 165 discharge test cycles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The proposed first-order ECM for LiB cell
ce:section.ce:para:
The ECM represents the inside dynamic behavior of the battery using
R , L
and C circuit elements as well as the nonlinear relationship between open circuit voltage (OCV) and SoC of LiB. If we refer to more dynamic characteristics of the battery at different frequency ranges, the dynamic model of LiB will be more complex. Based on the previous studies presented in references [28,30], in this paper, we use a first-order ECM. To ensure a compromise between the complexity of the model and the accuracy when estimating SoC, in this study, the ECM of the cell will ignore the high-frequency component L , the
C
e
component, and the diffusion process. The model will only refer to the main characteristic parameters of the cell. The resistances
R
e
,
impedance
R
ct
C
ct
and
Q
rate
are the important parameters that significantly affect the aging phenomenon of the cell during use. Besides that, the model also needs to consider hysteresis of cell voltage during charge and discharge; relationship between OCV and SoC by temperatures.

A proposed first-order ECM for the cell is described in Fig. 4, where
OCV
z
t
is the function of OCV by SoC;
z ( t )
is SoC; h represents the voltage delay between the charge and discharge process;
R
e
( T )
represents the resistance of the SEI layer; the
R
ct
( T )
/ / C
ct
( T )
describes the surface kinetic effects on the electrodes, where
R
ct
( T )
represents the resistance of electric charge transmission, and these parameters are temperature-dependent. This model reflects the important characteristics of the cell, including the non-linear relationship between the OCV and SoC, the temperature dependence of the resistances of the electrolyte layer and electric charge transmission, diffusion voltage phenomenon and voltage delay. The typical temperature-dependent relationship between OCV and SoC is plotted in Fig. 5.

Let
Δ T
be the sampling time, the equation representing the current flowing through the
R
ct
( T )

( T )
branch in the cell model is discretized as Eq. (2) follows:
(2)

and time constant

, the output voltage is depend on resistances of SEI and the charge transfer, and also the component

z ( k )
.

Eq. (10) is the model of the cell dynamic state, with the cell voltage as output and the current flowing through the cell as input. The matrices of the model depend on the
SoH
k
or current capacity
Q

R

and temperature. The voltage of the cell in the rest state (no current flow) will converge to

. The parameters

are determined based on experimental data, and all parameters in this model are temperature-dependent.

To determine the parameters of the model (10), two stages need to be performed: the first is to determine the component
OCV
z ( k )
as a function of operating temperature, and the second is to determine the dynamic parameters
M ( T ) ,
R

( T )
and
R
ct
( T )
C
ct
( T )
which also depend on the temperature. A new cell is used to determine the parameters of the model (10).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determining the relationship between OCV and SoC with respect to temperature
ce:section.ce:para:
To determine the relationship
OCV
z ( k )
of a battery cell, the cell needs to be discharged slowly, then charged slowly correspondingly under the same examined temperature conditions. The experimental data collection process is carried out for 5 operating points corresponding to temperatures of 5° C, 15° C, 25° C, 35° C and 45° C. At each temperature, the experimental data collection needs to be normalized to room temperature of 25° C. The slow charging and discharging process is carried out to eliminate the heat generation phenomenon in the cell, to ensure that the measured data are performed evenly at the examined temperature. During the data collection process, the following parameters need to be measured and recorded: measurement time
( s )
; current
( A )
; voltage across the battery terminals
( V )
; charge capacity
( Ah )
; discharge capacity
( Ah )
; charge energy
( Ah )
; discharge energy (Ah); voltage change rate (
dV / dt
). The four testing steps are shown in Fig. 6.

Let
X
k
d , T
,
X
k
c , T
be the measured quantity X at the sampling point k of the respective discharge and charge process at steps 1 and 4 at temperature T , X is the measured quantity consisting of i ,
v , C , W
and
dv
dt
which are current, voltage, capacity, energy, and voltage change rate, respectively.

The standard Coulomb efficiency at 250 C, denoted as
η ( 2
5

, is determined by the following formula:
(11)

T

. Coulomb efficiency is usually approximately equal to 1.

Determine the depth of discharge (DOD), which is a parameter that measures the discharged capacity of the battery, DOD = 100 % means that the battery is fully discharged. DOD at the time
t

Q

at temperature T is equal to the DoD at the end of step 4.

The SoC at time
t

is determined by formula (14).
(14)

We can see that the SoC at the end of step 1 must be 0 %, and the SoC at the end of step 4 must be 100 %. Fig. 7 illustrates
OCV
z ( k )
corresponding to steps 1 and 4, where the lowest curve is
OCV
z ( k )
corresponding to the discharge process in step 1, the uppermost curve is
OCV
z ( k )
corresponding to the charge process in step 4, and the dashed line in the middle is the approximate
OCV
z ( k )
.

Suppose that the voltage changes between the discharge curve and the charge curve at the 50 % SoC point, and the resistance changes linearly in those two ranges, which are the ranges from 0 % to 50 % SoC, and from 50 % to 100 % SoC. By combining the OCV relationships by temperature, we can establish the relationship between SoC and OCV by temperature T as follows:
(15)
OCV ( z (
t

Δ

( z (

) )
is the linear correction coefficient according to temperature, which is a function of
z (

) .
.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determine the dynamic parameters of the model according to temperature
ce:section.ce:para:
The remaining model parameters that need to be determined are:
γ , M ( T ) ,
R
e
( T ) ,
R
ct
( T )
C
ct
( T ) ,
R
ct
( T )
. To determine these parameters, dynamic data needs to be collected from the battery through experiments, including the Urban Dynamometer Driving Schedule (UDDS) current profile [33]. The data collection process is similar to the case of determining the relationship between OCV and SoC at different temperatures described above. The experimental process to collect data is illustrated in Fig. 8.
•
Step 1: Calculate
R
ct
C

using subspace identification techniques [34].
•
Step 2: Select the appropriate value of γ based on prior knowledge of the cell.

The determined parameters of the ECM of the cell are for the test temperatures of 5° C, 15° C, 25° C, 35° C, 45° C. The parameters of the LiB cell SAMSUNG, INR18650–25R Lithium Ion 18650, 2500mAh. Capacity: 2500 mAh; Weight: 49 g; Warranty: 1 Year; Cycle life: 1000 times;
V

= 2.5 V;

= 4.25 V.

Figs. 9–13.

The

5

,

,

, and

C
, which are reflected by root mean square error (RMSE) of 2.6 mV, 3.7 mV, 1.3 mV, and 1.8 mV, respectively.

Fig. 14 shows the relationship of the model parameters of the battery cell and temperature, including
Q
rate
, M ( T ) ,
R
ct
( T )

( T ) ,

( T )
. These parameters vary significantly by the working temperature of cell. The battery capacity
Q
rate
has the highest value of
2.19 Ah
at a temperature of
19
0
C
, but it decreases rapidly to
2.15 Ah
at temperatures higher than

C
. As the temperature increases, the resistance
R
e
decreases. The parameters
M ( T )
also vary similarly with temperature. The time constants

( T )
and
C
ct
( T )
increase rapidly with temperature, and the resistance
R
ct
( T )
in the

ct
( T ) / /

ct
( T )
branch decreases correspondingly with temperature.

Comparing the output voltage of the model in two test cases, OCV determination test and parameter determination test, with the actual voltage at
45
0
C
as shown in Fig. 15, the model and experimental deviation at
45
0
C
is represented in Fig. 16.

It can be seen that Fig. 16 compares the output voltage of the cell model with the determined parameters from the experimental data in two cases (experimental data to determine the funtion of OCV and SoC, and experimental data to determine the remaining parameters), showing that the output voltage of the cell model is almost identical to the output voltage of the two sets of experimental data at
45
0
C
. This deviation has an average value of 0 and is within the range of ±0.02 V. Considering the smallest voltage value of
3.7 V
, this deviation accounts for approximately 0.54 % as shown in Fig. 16.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SoC estimation of cell using SPKF based on ECM of lib cell considering the influence of temperature and aging parameters

ce:section.ce:para: SPKF is a estimation methods for nonlinear systems. This method provides more accurate state estimation for high-order nonlinear systems with computation complexity equivalent to Extend Kalman filter (EKF). SPKF is easier to implement because it does use the calculation of Jacobian matrix [35]. For EKF, the nonlinear dynamics of the system are linearized at the sampling time. As observed, the relationship between SoC and open circuit voltage in the region of SoC <30 % and >80 % is highly nonlinear, so the SoC estimation using SPKF is more exact than using EKF [17],[35–37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: SoC estimation for LiB cell using SPKF based on ECM of LiB cell considering the operating temperature and aging parameters
ce:section.ce:para:
From ECM of the cell in part 1, considering the case where the current and voltage measurements of the cell have measurement noise, the dynamic model (10) becomes:
(19)

w

and covariance matrices

, respectively. The state estimation algorithm for the system (19) is described specifically in documents [29, 30]. For ease of representation, we denote the variables at sampling time k in the form of
x

is determined by the Table 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results of SoC estimation considering the influence of the aging
ce:section.ce:para:
The results of estimating the SoC taking into account the influence of SoH are presented in this section. The estimation of SoC will be carried out for the charging process in some typical cycles during the usage process, which are cycles 1, 50, 100, and 150. Cycle 1, the battery has been used for a short time with SoH = 87.04 %; the 50th charging cycle has SoH = 85.41 %; the 100th charging cycle has SoH = 70.25 %; the 150th charging cycle has SoH = 62.77 %. The cells were initially charged from different SoC, and the charging process was carried out as follows: the cell was charged with a constant current mode with a charging current of 1.8 A until the cell voltage reached 4.2 V, then switched to the constant voltage charging mode until the current through the cell was 0, at which point the charging process ended. The results of estimating the SoC for the charging cycles are shown in
Figs. 18–21.

Fig. 17 shows the estimated SoC results for the corresponding cell with the first charging cycle, where the cell has SoH = 87.04 %, electrochemical impedance
R
ct
= 0.07012 Ω
, and
R
e
= 0.04532 Ω
. With an initial SoC of 70.06 %, after about 8000 s, the cell is fully charged with SoC = 100 %. The constant current charging process takes place within about 800 s, and the amount of SoC increased during the constant current period accounts for 66.27 % of the total 29.94 % increase in SoC during the entire charging process. The amount of SoC increased during the constant voltage charging period accounts for 33.73 %.

Fig. 18 shows the estimated SoC results for the corresponding cell with the 50th charging cycle, where the cell has SoH = 85.41 %, electrochemical impedance
R
ct
= 0.0701 Ω
, and
R
e
= 0.0495 Ω
. With an initial SoC of 25.2 %, after about 10,000 s, the cell is fully charged with SoC = 100 %. The constant current charging process takes place within about 3000 s, and the amount of SoC increased during the constant current period accounts for 81.95 % of the total 74.8 % increase in SoC during the entire charging process. The amount of SoC increased during the constant voltage charging period accounts for 18.05 %.

Fig. 19 shows the estimated SoC results for the corresponding cell with the 100th charging cycle, where the cell has SoH = 70.25 %, electrochemical impedance
R
ct
= 0.08 Ω
, and
R
e
= 0.0594 Ω
. With an initial SoC of 21.5 %, after about 10,000 s, the cell is fully charged with SoC = 100 %. The constant current charging process takes place within about 2100 s, and the amount of SoC increased during the constant current period accounts for 78.92 % of the total 78.5 % increase in SoC during the entire charging process. The amount of SoC increased during the constant voltage charging period accounts for 21.08 %.

Fig. 20 shows the SoC estimation results for the cell corresponding to the 150th charge cycle, where the cell has a SoH value of 62.77 %, an electrochemical impedance of
R
ct
= 0.08 Ω
, and
R
e
= 0.05855 Ω
. With an initial SoC of 24.5 %, the cell was fully charged after about 8000 s with a SoC of 100 %. The constant current charging process took place for about 1800 s, during which the amount of SoC increased by 70.76 % of the total 75.75 % SoC increase during the entire charging process. The amount of SoC increase during the constant voltage charging period accounted for 29.24 %.

Fig. 21 shows the difference in SoC corresponding to SoH, particularly noticeable for cycles 50, 100, and 150 with similar initial SoC charging processes. This figure shows that accurate SoC estimation depends heavily on SoH. Therefore, accurate SoC estimation requires consideration of the SoH parameter in the model. The newer the cell, the less the increase in SoC during the constant current charging process compared to the older cell. The older the cell, i.e., the smaller the SoH, the more the increase in SoC during the constant current charging period (see Table 2). The time to fully charge the cell decreases with age, and vice versa. The average charging rat %/s of SoC increases with decreasing SoH, as shown in Table 3. Specifically, for a cell with SoC = 87.04 %, the average charging rate is 0.0038 %/s, and when the SoH of the cell decreases to 70.25 %, this rate almost doubles to 0.0073 %/s, especially when the SoH of the cell is 62.77 %, the average charging rate is 0.0092 %/s. Fig. 22 plots the changing of cell’s temperature at charge cycles 1, 50, 100 150 correspond respectively to SoH = 87.04 %, 85.41 %, 70.25 %, and 62.77 %. For the cell at the 100 and 150 test charge cycles, corresponding to SoH 70.25 % and 62.77 % the heat increase is nearly 40 C; while at the first test cycle, the heat increase is 2.5° C.

This paper has presented the SoC estimation of a LiB cell using the SPKF filter, taking into account the parameters describing the aging process of the cell, namely the SoH parameter and the resistances of the electrochemical process in the equivalent circuit model of the cell. The use of the SPKF filter allows for consideration of the non-linearity of the relationship between open circuit voltage and SoC in the ECM model. The parameters describing the cell aging process used in ECM permit representation more accurate of the dynamics of the cell change during operation. The parameters of ECM model were established for new cells at different temperatures based on experimental data from different scenarios. The accuracy of the model was evaluated by comparing the OCV output voltage of the model and the measured voltage from the experimental process, and the voltage error between the OCV output of the model and the output voltage of the test cell was within the confidence interval of
± 0.02 V
. Based on proposed first-order ECM, the paper then presented the SoC estimation of a battery cell based on the SPKF filter. The results of estimating SoC for some cases of cells with different degrees of aging corresponding to different SoH values show the dependence of the amount of SoC increase during the constant current charging process, charging time, and average SoC increase rate during charging on the SoH or aging level of the cell. Estimating SoC with consideration of SoH increases accuracy, especially for used cells. The results of estimating SoC show that the amount of SoC increase during constant current charging is less for newer cells than for older cells. As the cell ages, i.e., SoH decreases, the amount of SoC increase during the constant current charging period is higher. The charging time will be shorter for older cells and longer for newer cells. As the cell ages, the average SoC increase rate during charging
( % / s )
is higher compared to newer cells.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported in part by the Thai Nguyen University of Technology, Viet nam.

J. Power Sources

2019 6th Int. Conf. Instrum. Control Autom. (ICA) Bdg. Indones.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N.A. Azis, E. Joelianto and A. Widyotriatmo, "State of Charge (SoC) and State of Health (SoH) Estimation of Lithium-Ion Battery Using Dual Extended Kalman Filter Based on Polynomial Battery Model," 2019 6th International Conference on Instrumentation, Control, and Automation (ICA), Bandung, Indonesia, 2019, pp. 88–93, doi: 10.1109/ICA.2019.8916734.

Prot. Control Mod. Power Syst.

Int. J. Electrochem. Sci.

Reliab. Eng. Syst. Saf.

Energy
